plainMeaning: In this final section the worshipper admits all the ways they held back spiritual energy — skipping or shortening their daily duties, neglecting study, charity, pilgrimage and care for fellow believers — sweeps up every remaining fault by mind, speech and body, and seals the whole confession with the renunciation-vow of limited use.
recitation: 4. vīryācāre chatī śakte chha āvaśyaka samyak sācavyā na hoya. chatī veḷāe posaha sāmāyika kīdhuṁ na hoya. beṭhāṁ vāndaṇāṁ dīdhāṁ hoya. kāussagga adhikā adhiko vadhāryo na hoya. chatī śakte tapa kīdhuṁ na hoya. dveṣa karato koi vāryo na hoya. navuṁ paḍhatāṁ, āgaluṁ gaṇatāṁ, artha vicāra pūchatāṁ kahetāṁ āḷasa kīdhuṁ hoya. chatī sāmagrīe dāna dīdhuṁ na hoya. devayātrā guruyātrā kīdhī na hoya. sahāmmītaṇī bhakti sācavī na hoya. dharmavaṁtanī āpadā bhāṅgī na hoya. aneruṁ e vīryācāra viṣe. ॥ 4 ॥ aneruṁ je kāṁi jinavacana viruddha mana, vacana kāyāe karī karaṇa, karāvaṇa, anumatie karī samācaryuṁ hoya. te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ ॥ paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe, kiccāṇamakaraṇe paḍikkamaṇaṁ. asaddahaṇe ya tahā, vivarīya parūvaṇā e ya ॥ 1 ॥ desāvagāsiaṁ, uvabhogaṁ paribhogaṁ paccakkhāmi. annatthaṇābhogeṇaṁ, sahassāgāreṇaṁ, mahattarāgāreṇaṁ savvasamāhivattiyāgāreṇaṁ vosirāmi ॥

wordByWord.gloss: in the conduct of [right] energy — the fifth ācāra, the right channelling of spiritual strength into practice (locative)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vīrya (energy, valour) + ācāra (conduct), in the locative case
wordByWord.notes: The last of the five ācāras; the four preceding verses confessed jñāna-, darśana-, [cāritra-] and tapācāra.

wordByWord.etymology: from Sanskrit sat- ('being, existing'); Old Gujarati feminine chatī
wordByWord.notes: Recurs with śakte, veḷāe and sāmagrīe — each time it means 'although the capacity / occasion / means was actually there'. This 'despite ability' framing is the moral hinge of vīryācāra.
Recurs; 'chatī śakte' = 'though one was perfectly able'.

wordByWord.gloss: essential duties — the six obligatory observances (sāmāyika, caturviṁśati-stava, vandana, pratikramaṇa, kāyotsarga, pratyākhyāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit āvaśyaka ('that which must be done')
Gujarati sācavavuṁ ('to keep, protect'); past participle, masc. pl.

wordByWord.notes: Recurs throughout; with the optative hoya it forms 'may not have …', the hypothetical-confessional mood of the whole atiyāra.

wordByWord.gloss: [it] may have been / might be the case
wordByWord.notes: Recurs; the optative deliberately keeps each fault hypothetical — 'should it have happened' — so the reciter confesses without needing to have committed each act.

wordByWord.gloss: pauṣadha — a day-long ascetic retreat the layperson keeps like a monk
sāmāyika — the ~48-minute vow of equanimity (1st āvaśyaka)

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati kīdhuṁ, past participle of karavuṁ ('to do')
wordByWord.notes: Recurs (kīdhuṁ / kīdhī).
The fault is doing vāndaṇā seated; the dvādaśāvarta vandana requires the prescribed standing/bowing postures, so a seated salute is a lapse of effort.

wordByWord.gloss: vāndaṇā — the formal twelve-gesture veneration of the guru (3rd āvaśyaka)

wordByWord.notes: Note: this clause lacks 'na' — the fault here is that one DID perform vāndaṇā (improperly, seated).

wordByWord.gloss: kāyotsarga — disciplined bodily stillness/meditation (5th āvaśyaka), measured in Logassa-counts
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit kāya-utsarga ('abandonment of the body') → Prakrit kāussagga
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling kāussagga (double-s), preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: more and more, by an extra measure
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit adhika ('more, additional'), reduplicated for intensity
wordByWord.notes: The lapse is failing to lengthen the kāyotsarga beyond the minimum when one could have.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati vadhāravuṁ ('to increase'), past participle masc. sg.
Gujarati karavuṁ, present participle masc. sg.
Sanskrit √vṛ / vāraṇa ('to ward off, restrain'); Gujarati vāravuṁ, past participle
wordByWord.notes: The fault: not using one's energy to dissuade someone acting out of malice.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit paḍhaï ('reads'), from Sanskrit paṭhati; Gujarati present participle
wordByWord.notes: Refers to svādhyāya — taking up fresh scriptural study.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit gaṇ ('to count, recite'); Gujarati present participle
wordByWord.notes: Here = revising committed text, keeping it 'live' by repetition.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit kathayati ('tells'); Gujarati kahevuṁ, present participle

wordByWord.notes: Note this clause uses kīdhuṁ HOY (no 'na'): the fault is that sloth WAS indulged — the one positive admission in the study series.

wordByWord.gloss: with the [requisite] means / resources at hand
charity, giving (to ascetics, the needy, the Saṅgha)
pilgrimage / procession to the Jina-temple and images
journeying to visit and serve the guru

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati kīdhī, past participle fem. agreeing with yātrā

wordByWord.gloss: belonging to a co-religionist (sādharmika), a fellow Jain on the same path
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāhammī (Sanskrit sādharmika, 'of the same dharma') + Gujarati genitive -taṇī
wordByWord.notes: Sādharmika-bhakti — honouring and supporting one's fellow believers — is a cardinal śrāvaka duty.

wordByWord.gloss: of a religious / virtuous person (one endowed with dharma)

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bhaṅga ('breaking'); Gujarati bhāṅgavuṁ, past participle fem.
wordByWord.notes: 'āpadā bhāṅgī na hoya' = one may have failed to break (relieve) a virtuous person's distress.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati aneruṁ ('another, additional'), from Sanskrit anya
wordByWord.notes: The standard sweep-up word that closes every vow-section, gathering unnamed faults.
Recurs (also in 'parūvaṇā e ya').

wordByWord.gloss: concerning, regarding, in the matter of
the word / teaching of the Jina

wordByWord.notes: First member of the mana–vacana–kāyā triad along which every fault is reckoned.
Appears also inflected as 'vacane' (by speech).
Recurs as the instrumental linker 'by mind, speech and body'.
First of the karaṇa–kārāvaṇa–anumati triad of agency.

wordByWord.gloss: by approving / consenting to [a deed done by others]
wordByWord.notes: Completes the threefold complicity: doing, causing, condoning.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sarva → Prakrit savva → Old Gujarati savi

wordByWord.gloss: micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ — 'may my misdeed come to nothing' (the Prakrit seal of repentance)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit; Sanskrit mithyā me duṣkṛtam ('my wrong deed be false/void')
wordByWord.notes: Defined in the preface glossary; here it seals not just vīryācāra but the entire five-ācāra confession.
Opens the sealing gāthā that classifies all transgression into four heads.
Fault 1: doing what is forbidden.

wordByWord.gloss: in the not-doing of prescribed duties
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kicca (Sanskrit kṛtya, 'duty') + akaraṇa ('non-doing'), locative — sandhi kiccāṇaṁ + akaraṇe
wordByWord.notes: Fault 2: omitting what ought to be done.
The gāthā's predicate: all four faults are the very ground for which pratikramaṇa is performed.

wordByWord.gloss: in disbelief / want of faith [in the teaching]
wordByWord.notes: Fault 3: lapse of conviction.
Recurs twice in the gāthā.
Fault 4: teaching the doctrine wrongly. The four — forbidden-done, prescribed-omitted, disbelief, false teaching — together define what pratikramaṇa atones.

wordByWord.gloss: deśāvagāśika — the vow restricting the day's space and use to a small compass (the 10th lay vow)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit, from Sanskrit deśa ('region') + avakāśa ('scope, room')
wordByWord.notes: The closing pratyākhyāna; in this edition the deśāvagāśika formula seals the bṛhad atiyāra.

wordByWord.gloss: upabhoga — things used once (food, drink, etc.)
paribhoga — things used repeatedly (clothes, dwelling, etc.)

wordByWord.notes: The performative verb of every pratyākhyāna (6th āvaśyaka).

wordByWord.gloss: except through inadvertence / forgetfulness (the first standard 'exception', āgāra)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit annattha ('otherwise, except') + aṇābhoga (Sanskrit anābhoga, 'inattention'), instrumental
wordByWord.notes: Begins the āgāra-clauses that keep an involuntary slip from breaking the vow.

wordByWord.gloss: except by a sudden, unintended act (the 'sahasā' exception)
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling sahassā- (doubled s), preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: except under a weightier obligation / greater necessity
except for the sake of complete equanimity / well-being (e.g. grave illness)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva ('all') + samāhi (Sanskrit samādhi) + vattiya (Sanskrit pratyaya, 'on account of') + āgāra, instrumental

wordByWord.notes: The closing act of renunciation that seals the whole bṛhad atiyāra.

literalTranslation: In the conduct of energy: though able, the six essential duties may not have been kept rightly; when the occasion was there, pauṣadha and sāmāyika may not have been done; the vāndaṇā may have been given while seated; the kāyotsarga may not have been extended ever longer; though able, austerity may not have been done; one acting out of malice may not have been restrained; in learning new scripture, revising the old, asking and explaining the meaning, sloth may have been indulged; though the means were present, charity may not have been given; pilgrimage to the temple and journey to the guru may not have been made; devotion to a co-religionist may not have been maintained; a virtuous person's distress may not have been relieved — and whatever else there is regarding energy-conduct. ॥4॥ And whatever else, contrary to the Jina's word, by mind, speech or body, by doing, causing or consenting, may have been committed — for all of that, by mind, speech and body, may my misdeed be void. ॥ Transgression lies in doing the forbidden, in not doing the prescribed, and likewise in disbelief and in contrary preaching — [these are what] pratikramaṇa [addresses]. ॥1॥ I renounce single-use and repeated-use enjoyment within the deśāvagāśika limit; saving inadvertence, a sudden act, a greater necessity and the need of full equanimity, I cast it off.
idiomaticTranslation: Now I turn to right energy — vīryācāra. Though I had the strength, did I truly keep the six daily duties? When the moment came, did I take up pauṣadha and sāmāyika, or let them slip? Did I bow the vāndaṇā properly, or salute slumped in my seat? Did I stretch my kāyotsarga that little longer, or stop at the least? Did I fast as I was able? When someone burned with spite, did I have the heart to restrain them? In studying afresh, in keeping my old learning alive, in asking after meanings and passing them on — was I instead seized by sloth? With the means in my hands, did I give? Did I make the pilgrimage to the temple, the journey to my guru? Did I cherish and serve my fellow believers, and break the misfortune of the devout? — for all this, and whatever else of energy I let slacken. And whatever I have done against the Jina's word — in thought, word or deed, doing it, having it done, or quietly approving — for every bit of it, by mind, speech and body: micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ. Transgression is fourfold: doing the forbidden, omitting the prescribed, faltering in faith, teaching falsely — and that is what this turning-back undoes. So, within my deśāvagāśika bound, I renounce all use and enjoyment beyond it; barring honest oversight, sudden accident, grave necessity or the demand of my own peace at the end — I let it go.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse is the keystone of the five-ācāra coda. Having confessed knowledge, faith and austerity, the reciter reaches vīryācāra — the conduct of energy — and its diagnostic word is chatī, 'though present': every fault is 'though I had the strength / the occasion / the means.' Vīrya is not raw capacity but its right deployment, so the only sin worth naming is unspent capacity. The litany is deliberately exhaustive: ritual (the six āvaśyakas, pauṣadha, sāmāyika, a properly-bowed vāndaṇā, a kāyotsarga stretched 'ever longer', fasting), study (taking up the new, revising the old, asking and teaching the meaning — where, tellingly, the verb drops its 'na': sloth was actually indulged), and active charity toward others (dāna with means in hand, pilgrimage to temple and guru, service to one's sādharmika, and the relief of a devout person's distress). Then the verse pivots from inventory to closure. The aneruṁ … micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ refrain gathers not only the unnamed vīrya-faults but, positioned last, everything 'contrary to the Jina's word' across all five ācāras, reckoned along the two classic axes — agency (doing / causing / condoning) and instrument (mind / speech / body). The sealing gāthā 'paḍisiddhāṇaṁ karaṇe…' then supplies the theory behind the whole confession: every transgression reduces to four — doing the forbidden, omitting the prescribed, disbelief, and false teaching — and these four are precisely what pratikramaṇa exists to reverse. Finally the deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna turns reflection into resolve: confession (looking back) is completed by renunciation (binding the day ahead), with its four āgāras protecting the vow against involuntary breach.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Vīryācāra rests on the Jain teaching that the soul's innate energy (vīrya) is obstructed by vīryāntarāya-karma; spiritual progress is the unveiling of that energy against pramāda (heedlessness). Hence the verse measures sin not by deeds done but by ability withheld — a confession of omission. The closing gāthā is the doctrinally weightiest line: its four-fold scheme (pratiṣiddha-karaṇa, kṛtya-akaraṇa, aśraddhāna, viparīta-prarūpaṇā) is the classical definition of what constitutes atichāra/transgression in the Āvaśyaka tradition, and it explains the verb pratikramaṇa itself — 'turning back' from exactly these four. The deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna anchors the sixth āvaśyaka (renunciation): looking-back (ālocanā) is incomplete without looking-forward (pratyākhyāna), and the standard āgāras (anābhoga, sahasā, mahattara, savva-samāhi) embody the Jain insistence that a vow binds intention, not accident.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For today's reciter this verse is a conscience-audit of wasted capacity: not 'what wrong did I do?' but 'what good was within my power that I left undone?' — the skipped sāmāyika, the half-hearted bow, the study postponed to sloth, the charity unmade though the wallet was full, the struggling fellow-believer unhelped. Reciting the 'paḍisiddhāṇaṁ' gāthā gives a portable four-point checklist for any vow; and ending on the deśāvagāśika vow trains the habit of sealing reflection with a concrete commitment for the hours ahead, exceptions honestly acknowledged.

crossReferences.text: Āvaśyaka Niryukti (the four-fold definition of atichāra: pratiṣiddha-karaṇa, kṛtya-akaraṇa, aśraddhāna, viparīta-prarūpaṇā)

Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.), the five-ācāra confession sealing the Bṛhad Atiyāra

notes: The Gujarati prose is rendered from the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha edition; mātrā, anusvāra and the book's spellings (kāussagga, sahassāgāreṇaṁ, sādharmika as 'sahāmmī') are preserved verbatim, not normalized. 'aneruṁ … micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ' here serves double duty: it closes vīryācāra (4) AND, in the following sentence, sweeps up all conduct 'contrary to the Jina's word', sealing the entire five-ācāra confession — translators sometimes split these; I have kept them as the text presents them. The four āgāras of the deśāvagāśika pratyākhyāna match the standard pratyākhyāna-āgāra set; their precise legal scope varies slightly across commentaries. The 'paḍisiddhāṇaṁ' gāthā is widely attributed to the Āvaśyaka Niryukti tradition; exact gāthā-number attributions differ between editions, so I cite it by tradition rather than a single numbered line. Token glosses for recurring particles (na, hoya, ya, karī) are given once with a note that they repeat.
